does: This paper introduces a multi-role red teaming framework for systematically discovering and evaluating vulnerabilities in large language models, particularly their propensity to generate unfaithful or inaccurate outputs. The framework employs three components: target models under evaluation, attacker models that iteratively generate increasingly effective adversarial prompts, and jury models that rigorously assess response accuracy and consistency. Through empirical evaluation focused on faithfulness, the authors demonstrate that crafted adversarial prompts can increase attack success rates by up to 7.9% in question-answering tasks, exposing reliability weaknesses in LLM outputs. The work also reveals that structural constraints in summarization tasks measurably improve faithfulness, and shows that architectural design choices typically matter more than parameter scaling in determining vulnerability to adversarial attacks.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research in adversarial testing and robustness evaluation of neural language models, extending prior red teaming methodologies from other domains into the LLM context. It responds to increasing concerns about hallucination and unfaithfulness in large language models that have emerged as these systems are deployed in safety-critical applications. The paper connects to prior work on adversarial prompt generation and attack mechanisms against language models, while also drawing on evaluation frameworks for consistency and factual accuracy in NLP. The multi-role architecture advances beyond simpler adversarial testing approaches by incorporating systematic iteration and rigorous adjudication, building on insights from debate-based evaluation schemes and collaborative AI systems that use multiple models for improved assessment.
enables: This framework establishes foundations for more systematic vulnerability discovery in LLMs, enabling future work on targeted defenses against specific failure modes like hallucination and unfaithful generation. The empirical finding that architectural design choices outweigh scale suggests new research directions for designing inherently more reliable language models without simply increasing parameters. The approach could facilitate red teaming as a standard practice in LLM development pipelines, supporting safer deployment in high-stakes applications by uncovering reliability issues before deployment. The multi-role evaluation mechanism may generalize to assessing other critical properties beyond faithfulness, such as bias, safety, and alignment. The insights about structural constraints improving faithfulness could inform design principles for inherently more trustworthy model architectures and training approaches.
